summary: Doug Field, who left Tesla after overseeing Model 3 production, has returned to Apple to work in Bob Mansfield's Project Titan group. Field previously served as VP of Mac hardware engineering before joining Tesla in 2013, making him a natural fit for Apple's vehicle ambitions. Gruber argues this hire suggests Apple still intends to make actual vehicles rather than just autonomous systems for other manufacturers. He notes this aligns with Apple's longstanding approach of making the whole widget, not components.
key_points: Doug Field returned to Apple after leaving Tesla, joining the secretive Project Titan group under Bob Mansfield
Field's combined experience in Mac hardware at Apple and electric vehicle engineering at Tesla makes him uniquely suited for Titan
The hire suggests Apple still plans to build complete vehicles, contradicting reports that the project was scaled back to autonomous systems only
Apple's historical pattern is making whole products, not components for other companies
money_quotes: Apple's modus operandi has always been to make the whole widget. Apple makes products, not components.

I think it's an interesting hire, primarily because it suggests to me that Apple still has an interest in making actual vehicles, despite reports that the company has scaled back the project to merely make autonomous systems for inclusion in vehicles made by other companies.
That rumor never really made sense to me anyway — Apple's modus operandi has always been to make the whole widget.
Field returning to Apple also suggests to me that under Mansfield's leadership, the Titan project has regained its footing after its infamously rocky start.
I wouldn't read too much into any single hire, and the employee exchange program between Apple and Tesla continues to flow in both directions.
key_insight: Doug Field's return to Apple for Project Titan signals the company still intends to build complete vehicles, consistent with its philosophy of owning the entire product rather than supplying components.
